coredata.dc:title: MOKE study of Co/Ti/(Gd–Co) multilayers near the magnetic compensation state
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2005 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Svalov, A.V.
Fernandez, A.
Vas’kovskiy, V.O.
Kurlyandskaya, G.V.
Barandiarán, J.M.
Anton, R. Lopez
Tejedor, M.
coredata.dc:description: The magnetic behaviour of [Co/Ti/a-Gd0.36Co0.64/Ti]4/Co layered structures was studied by means of the transversal magneto-optic Kerr effect, with Ti spacers of different thicknesses. Within the Gd0.36Co0.64 amorphous layer, the Gd magnetic sub-lattice dominates at all investigated temperatures. The strong exchange interaction at the interfaces causes ‘macroscopic ferrimagnetic behaviour’ in the whole system. The existence of a magnetic compensation state in the complete multilayer system was manifested by the change of sign in the magneto-optic hysteresis loops and the increase of coercivity. The evolution of the critical field for a spin-orientation transition leading to a non-collinear magnetic state near the ‘compensation temperature’ can be described within molecular field theory. The influence of the non-magnetic spacer thickness on the characteristics of the compensation state is also discussed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ferrimagnet
Multilayers
Compensation state
Magneto-optical Kerr effect

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Principal diagram of the (Gd–Co)-aligned and Co-aligned phases with respect to the temperature range. In the (Gd–Co)-aligned phase, the moments of the Gd–Co layers are large and aligned with the external field. In the Co-aligned phase at T > T comp, the Gd–Co moments are small and the Co layers’ moments align with the field.
Surface images obtained by atomic force microscope for multilayered structures: [Co(7nm)/GdCo(24nm)]4/Co(7nm) (a); [Co(7nm)/Ti(1.5nm)/GdCo(24nm)/Ti(1.5nm)]4/Co(7nm) (b).

Normalized TMOKE hysteresis loops for a [Co(7nm)/GdCo(24nm)]4/Co(7nm) structure measured at different temperatures: 243K (a), 294K (b), 308K (c) and 320K (d). The loop height at 308K is chosen as the TMOKE signal unit. Only a few representative loops of those measured at different temperatures are shown. The breaks corresponding to the transition from a ferrimagnetic structure to a non-collinear state are indicated by arrows.

Normalized TMOKE hysteresis loops for [Co(7nm)/Ti(0.3nm)/GdCo(24nm)/Ti(0.3nm)]4/Co(7nm) structure measured at different temperatures: 272K (a), 291K (b), 295K (c) and 323K (d). The loop height at 272K is chosen as the TMOKE signal unit. Only a few representative loops of those measured at different temperatures are shown. The breaks corresponding to the transition from a ferrimagnetic structure to a non-collinear state are indicated by arrows.

Normalized Kerr loops for [Co(7nm)/Ti(0.5nm)/GdCo(24nm)/Ti(0.5nm)]4/Co(7nm) structure measured at different temperatures: 305K (a) and 363K (b). The loop height at 363K is chosen as the TMOKE signal unit. Only a few representative loops of those measured at different temperatures are shown. The breaks corresponding to transition from a ferrimagnetic structure to a non-collinear state are indicated by arrows.

Normalized Kerr loops for [Co(7nm)/Ti(1.5nm)/GdCo(24nm)/Ti(1.5nm)]4/Co(7nm) structure measured at different temperatures: 230K (a) and 345K (b). Only a few representative loops of those measured at different temperatures are shown.

Temperature dependence of coercivity for different multilayered structures: [Co(7nm)/GdCo(24nm)]4/Co(7nm) (a); [Co(7nm)/Ti(0.3nm)/GdCo(24nm)/Ti(0.3nm)]4/Co(7nm) (b); [Co(7nm)/Ti(0.5nm)/GdCo(24nm)/Ti(0.5nm)]4/Co(7nm) (c).

Temperature dependencies of the critical field H cr for different multilayered structures (the experimental data are shown as points and the results of model calculations are shown as solid lines): [Co(7nm)/GdCo(24nm)]4/Co(7nm) (a); [Co(7nm)/Ti(0.3nm)/GdCo(24nm)/Ti(0.3nm)]4/Co(7nm) (b); [Co(7nm)/Ti(0.5nm)/GdCo(24nm)/Ti(0.5nm)]4/Co(7nm) (c).

Temperature dependence of magnetization for [Co(7nm)/GdCo(24nm)]4/Co(7nm) multilayers: (a) measured at external magnetic field of 150Oe (1) and 500Oe (2); temperature dependence of saturation magnetization for a Gd0.36Co0.64 film (b).

article.ce:title: MOKE study of Co/Ti/(Gd–Co) multilayers near the magnetic compensation state

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Departamento de Física, Universidad de Oviedo, Avenida Calvo Sotelo s/n, 33007 Oviedo, Asturias, Spain

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Ural State University, Institute of Physics and Applied Mathematics, Lenin Avenue 51, 620083 Ekaterinburg, Russia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Departamento de Electricidad y Electrónica, Facultad de Ciencia y Tecnología, Universidad del País Vasco, UPV/EHU, P.O. Box 644, 48080 Bilbao, Spain

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The magnetic behaviour of [Co/Ti/a-Gd0.36Co0.64/Ti]4/Co layered structures was studied by means of the transversal magneto-optic Kerr effect, with Ti spacers of different thicknesses. Within the Gd0.36Co0.64 amorphous layer, the Gd magnetic sub-lattice dominates at all investigated temperatures. The strong exchange interaction at the interfaces causes ‘macroscopic ferrimagnetic behaviour’ in the whole system. The existence of a magnetic compensation state in the complete multilayer system was manifested by the change of sign in the magneto-optic hysteresis loops and the increase of coercivity. The evolution of the critical field for a spin-orientation transition leading to a non-collinear magnetic state near the ‘compensation temperature’ can be described within molecular field theory. The influence of the non-magnetic spacer thickness on the characteristics of the compensation state is also discussed.

ce:section.ce:para: Magnetic layered systems have attracted much attention recently, both for their fundamental interest as well as for possible technological applications in magnetic storage devices and sensors [1,2]. Multilayers consisting of rare-earth (RE) and transition metal (TM) components form an interesting family within these systems. The antiferromagnetic interlayer coupling in RE/TM structures gives rise to a variety of magnetic configurations [3]. Characteristic ferrimagnetic behaviour, such as compensation phenomena of the magnetization and low-field spin-flop transitions have been observed [4–10] and explained in terms of molecular field theory [8,11–14] in RE/TM multilayers. The magnetization process of RE/TM multilayers was first described by Camley and Tilley [11], as comprising three main phases: (a) the RE-aligned phase, where the RE magnetic moments are parallel to the magnetic field and TM moments are antiparallel; (b) the TM-aligned phase, with TM moments in the field direction and RE moments antiparallel to it; (c) the twisted phase, where RE and TM moments are away from the field direction when the applied field exceeds the critical value H cr.

RE/TM structures containing non-magnetic spacers between magnetic layers have not been studied in as much detail. Nevertheless, interesting peculiarities have been found for such systems: decrease of the strength of the coupling between magnetic layers with the increase of spacer thickness [15,16]; anomalous hysteresis loops [17]; biquadratic interlayer exchange coupling [18]; spin-glass-like behaviour of the magnetization in low fields [19].

Real RE/TM multilayers are often far from ideal planar structures with perfect interfaces. For example, the existence of interdiffusion between the Co and the Gd layers was shown in the case of Gd/Co multilayers, which led to Co alloying throughout the Gd layer up to composition near the eutectic point (Gd0.63Co0.37) [20]. In addition, the Curie points of many RE components occur at a range of rather low temperatures, which makes them less attractive for applications. In technological applications, it is better to replace the RE layers by RE-rich RE–TM alloy layers [21]. The RE–TM alloy films are, in general, amorphous and present high structural stability, magnetic ordering temperatures which are far higher than room temperature, and monotonous change of the main magnetic characteristics as a function of composition. Interesting aspects to be studied in TM/(RE–TM) multilayers include, therefore, the exchange interaction between RE and RE–TM alloy layers and the magnetic properties of these structures, as well as the effect of non-magnetic spacers.

In the present study we describe the magnetic behaviour of [Co/Ti/Gd0.36Co0.64/Ti]4/Co multilayers, in which the Gd0.36Co0.64 alloy plays the role of RE in classic RE/TM multilayers. Different thicknesses of the non-magnetic Ti layer have been used to study the effect of a non-magnetic spacer on the coupling between the pure Co and GdCo layers. Near the magnetic compensation temperature, the three different magnetic phases in multilayered structures were observed and studied.

The Gd0.36Co0.64 composition of the amorphous RE–TM layer was selected so that, near room temperature, its saturation magnetization would be dominated by the Gd. Because of the negative Gd–Co and positive Co–Co exchange interaction near the interface, the total magnetic moments of Gd–Co and Co layers are antiparallel (Fig. 1 ). Thus, [Co/Ti/(Gd–Co)/Ti]4/Co multilayers form a macroscopic ferrimagnet. At around room temperature the magnetization of the Gd0.36Co0.64 layers decreases quickly, while the magnetization of the Co layer remains almost constant. Therefore, the [Co/Ti/(Gd–Co)/Ti]4/Co multilayers can show a magnetic compensation state for which the net magnetization is zero at a temperature T comp. The Gd–Co moment dominates for T < T comp, while the Co moment does so at T > T comp (Fig. 1).

Ti was chosen for the spacers because it has very limited interdiffusion with Co layers at low temperatures [22] and low solubility in RE elements [23] and, therefore, has a minimal influence on the structural–chemical condition of Gd–Co and Co layers. Further confirmation of this fact was obtained in the present study.

[Co/Ti/Gd0.36Co0.64/Ti]4/Co multilayers were deposited using a three target RF sputtering system. Co and Gd0.36Co0.64 single layer films were also prepared for comparison. The background pressure was 1×10−6 mbar. Deposition was performed in Ar atmosphere with a partial pressure of 2×10−4 mbar. Samples were deposited on glass substrates at room temperature. A mosaic target was used for preparation of the Gd0.36Co0.64 layers. All multilayers had a fixed nominal thickness of 7nm for the Co layers and 24nm for the Gd0.36Co0.64 layers. The thickness of the Ti spacer layers (L Ti) ranged from 0.3 to 1.5nm.

The deposition rates of Gd–Co, Co and Ti thin films were previously calibrated by low angle X-ray reflectometry and atomic force microscopy, yielding 0.058nm/s for Co, 0.067nm/s for Gd–Co and 0.05nm/s for Ti, respectively. Thereafter, the thickness of all layers was estimated from the deposition time by using these deposition rates.

Multilayer deposition was always started and finished with a Co layer. Gd–Co single layer films were covered in situ with a 20nm thick layer of Ti in order to avoid oxidation. A magnetic field of 100Oe was applied during deposition in order to induce magnetic anisotropy in the plane of the film. According to the transmission electron microscopy data, the Gd–Co layers were amorphous and the Co layers were polycrystalline with FCC-crystallites of 5nm average diameter.

The temperature interval near room temperature was analysed because the magnetic compensation state of [Co/Ti/(Gd0.36Co0.64)/Ti]4/Co multilayers was expected at these temperatures. The magnetic properties were measured using a SQUID magnetometer and a vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM). In order to study the transverse magneto-optic Kerr effect (TMOKE), the sample was mounted in a nitrogen cryostat, and was measured at temperatures between 180 and 340K with a maximum field of 650Oe applied in the sample plane along the easy axis. Visible white light was used, with an estimated penetration depth of about 30–40nm [24], i.e. roughly one and a half times a multilayer period. For the wavelengths under consideration, the TMOKE signal comes almost entirely from the Co atoms [25]. Therefore, an inverted hysteresis loop can be observed when the Co magnetic moments are antiparallel to the external field. This occurs below the compensation temperature (T comp) when the magnetic moment of Gd0.36Co0.64 layers are parallel to the applied field and the magnetic moment of Co layers are antiparallel (see Fig. 1). Above T comp the moments of the Co layers are in the field direction and Gd0.36Co0.64 layers moments are antiparallel.

The magnetization process of [Co/Ti/Gd0.36Co0.64/Ti]4/Co multilayer at certain conditions can be characterized by the presence of the break point on the TMOKE loop in the critical field, H cr. In this field, the moments of both the Co and Gd–Co layers jump into directions deviated from the axis of the applied field. The latter field increase causes a bending of Co and GdCo layers moments towards each other.

Regarding the influence of Ti on the structural–chemical condition of Gd–Co and Co layers, Fig. 2 shows the surface structure of a multilayer without (Fig. 2a) and with (Fig. 2b) Ti spacers of 1.5nm thickness, obtained by atomic force microscopy (AFM). In both cases, the root-mean-square roughness (R rms) is quite low (0.6±0.1nm). Considering that the final surface roughness reflects, up to a certain point, the state of the internal interfaces in the multilayer, the relatively similar values for both samples seem to indicate that Ti layers, for L Ti =1.5nm, are continuous and flat, with little effect on the structure of the Co and Gd–Co layers.

For Gd–Co and Co single layer films, almost rectangular hysteresis loops with H c of about 10 and 60Oe, respectively (not shown here), were observed at all temperatures. Figs. 3–5
show the TMOKE hysteresis loops obtained at different temperatures on the [Co/Ti/(Gd–Co)/Ti]4/Co structures with L Ti =0, 0.3 and 0.5nm, respectively. In these samples, the sign of the Kerr signal changes from negative to positive when the temperature passes through the compensation temperature, T comp. This confirms that the saturation magnetization is dominated by the Co moments at temperatures above the compensation level, and by Gd moments at temperatures below it. The low field parts of hysteresis loops can be interpreted as being caused by the nucleation of magnetic domains and domain wall movement.

Fig. 6 shows the TMOKE hysteresis loops for L Ti =1.5nm. In this case, the hysteresis loops have very complicated shapes in all temperature ranges. The signal amplitude is practically constant. This behaviour is completely different from that of the other samples. The shape of each one of these loops can be interpreted as the sum of TMOKE signals from the layers of two different types. The magnetization process of the layer of one type can be independent of that for the other type of layer. At high field, Co magnetic moments of Gd–Co layers are antiparallel to the external field and Co moments of Co layers. Thus, the TMOKE signal from Co and Gd–Co layers compensate each other. When the external field decreases down to zero and changes the sign at low fields, the remagnetization process first occurs in Gd–Co layers which have low coercivity. This leads to an increase in the TMOKE signal. As the field increases, Co layer remagnetization appears, which leads to a decrease in the total TMOKE signal.
Fig. 7 shows the temperature dependence of coercivity (H c) for L Ti =0, 0.3 and 0.5nm. These are typical of ferrimagnets near the compensation state.

TMOKE loops for L Ti =0, 0.3 and 0.5nm show break points at certain temperatures and values of the external field (H cr), when the non-collinear magnetic phase sets in (Figs. 3b, c, 4b, c and 5). Note, that Gd0.36Co0.64 layer is a ferrimagnet by itself, but its H cr is of the order of hundreds of kilo Oersteds because we are far from its compensation temperature of 470K. Therefore, when a non-collinear state appears in the [Co/Ti/(Gd–Co)/Ti]4/Co multilayers, the Co and Gd magnetic moments remain antiparallel within the Gd–Co amorphous layers. Fig. 8 shows the temperature dependences of H cr for these structures. We were able to observe the spin-orientation transition only in a narrow temperature range, especially for samples with L Ti =0 and 0.3nm. On the one hand, in the vicinity of T comp the experimental definition of the H cr is complicated because values of H c and H cr are close to each other; as T comp is approached, coercivity grows, whereas H cr decreases. On the other hand, for temperatures far from T comp a strong increase of H cr occurs and its value exceeds the measuring limit of the equipment. Available data indicate a linear change of H cr with temperature.

One way of calculating the magnetic properties of RE/TM multilayers is to obtain spin configurations in the framework of molecular field theory, since these spin maps allow the calculation of several magnitudes, such as M(H), M(T), H cr(T) [11–13,26], which are well consistent with experimental data [8,27–29]. Such a method is necessary for RE/TM multilayers, where the exchange coupling constant between TM atoms (as given by the molecular field constant λ TM) is greater than the coupling (λ RE) between RE atoms by as much as one order of magnitude, and the absolute value of the antiferromagnetic exchange coupling between TM and RE atoms at the interface (λ) is intermediate between λ TM and λ RE [8,27–29]. This relationship between λ, λ TM and λ RE leads to the appearance of so-called twisted magnetic phase in the external field. This magnetic structure is formed basically in the RE layers, and can be characterized by angular and amplitude variations of the local magnetization [11].

In TM/(RE–TM) multilayers, such as these we are dealing with, no such great difference between the interlayer exchange coupling constants occurs. Moreover, the exchange within layers is similar or even higher than that between layers. Therefore, these multilayers are more similar to standard (non-layered) ferrimagnets.

Let us calculate H cr in terms of the phenomenological model for isotropic ferrimagnets. The properties of the multilayered structure depend on the surface effect and the anisotropy. In a first approximation, however, many features observed in Co/(Gd–Co) multilayers can be reproduced using a simple model which includes only the Zeeman energy in the applied field H and the exchange energy between the layers, i.e. disregarding the anisotropy. This leads to the following formula for the energy density:
(1)
ε = −
l
GdCo
M
GdCo
H
cos ⁡
θ 1
−
l
Co
M
Co
H
cos ⁡
θ 2
−
λ L
M
GdCo
M
Co
cos ⁡ (
θ 2
−
θ 1
)
where M GdCo and M Co are the magnetizations of the Gd–Co and Co layers, θ 1 and θ 2 are their angles with the external field; l GdCo = L Gd/L and l Co = L Co/L are their respective volume fractions; λ is the interlayer exchange coupling constant. Here, L GdCo and L Co are the total thickness of Gd–Co and Co layers, respectively, and L = L GdCo + L Co is the total thickness of the multilayer. Eq. (1) coincides with the energy density of a standard (non-layered) ferrimagnet with two sublattices, of magnetization M 1 = M GdCo l GdCo and M 2 = M Co l Co and exchange constant J = λ/L. Following refs. [30,31], for the standard ferrimagnet the critical field, in which there are deviations of both the M 1 and M 2 from the axis of the applied field, can be written as follow:
(2)
H
cr
= J |
M 1
−
M 2
|
The equation for H cr for multilayers studied in present work can be obtained by means of the corresponding substitution:
(3)
H
cr
=
λ L
|
l
GdCo
M
GdCo
( T ) −
l
Co
M
Co
( T ) |
The value shown in square brackets is the total magnetization of the sample at the field less than H cr. It can be determined from an experiment. Using the data for the magnetization presented in Fig. 9a (curve 1) one can describe satisfactorily the experimental temperature dependence of H cr for structures without Ti spacers by Eq. (3) with λ ≈−9×10−5 erg/G2 cm2 (Fig. 8a).

The value shown in square brackets can be calculated on the basis of M(T) data for Gd–Co layers. Fig. 9b shows experimental M(T) dependence for Gd0.36Co0.64 single layer film. Using these data, curve 1 in Fig. 9a can be obtained if we assume that the spontaneous magnetization of the Co layers does not change appreciably in the temperature interval considered, and M Co =1385G. This number is close to one for bulk Co (1420G). It is known that Co magnetization in thin films decreases as the film thickness decreases [32], therefore 1385G was considered as the magnetization value for the Co layers under consideration.
We must note that M(T) curves measured at different fields show slightly different behaviour, especially near T comp and the discrepancy is more appreciable below T comp (Fig. 9a).

Most probably, it is connected to the formation of different spin configurations above and below T comp at H > H cr. As was shown for Gd/Fe multilayers, the transition from an aligned to a twisted state is initiated at the surface if the minority moment corresponds to the surface, and in the interior when the majority moment corresponds to the surface [26,27]. Surface phase transition takes place at a lower field than that required for transition within the internal layers because of the specific conditions for exchange coupling at the surface. In our case, the outermost (surface and substrate) layers are the Co layers. The Co-aligned phase appears above T comp. Therefore, the canting starts in the interior at T > T comp. At T < T comp, the Gd-aligned phase occurs and one may expect transition into the non-collinear phase at lower fields. The temperature dependencies of the magnetization shown in Fig. 9a confirm this supposition: the difference between curve 1 and curve 2 is more pronounced below T comp, i.e. non-collinear state occurs in lower fields in this temperature interval. Near T comp surface non-collinear phase can arise in the fields, which are lower then H c. Most likely that the MOKE loop shown in Fig. 3a describes the magnetization reversal in the sample, in which the surface non-collinear phase is already exist. It is necessary to apply a higher field in order to cause the extension of the non-collinear phase from the surface toward the center of the sample. Perhaps this is the reason of the absence a “kink” on the MOKE curves at T < T comp for fields up to 650Oe.

Let us calculate H cr for L Ti =0.3 and 0.5nm. Taking into account that values of H c and H cr are close to each other near T comp, and therefore it is difficult to obtain correctly the M(T) dependence for multilayer structures in this temperature interval, let us obtain the value of the contribution in the square brackets in Eq. (3) not experimentally, but by means of the calculation on the basis of M(T) data for Gd–Co layers as described above for L Ti =0. We can see from Fig. 8 the H cr(T) experimental dependences can be satisfactorily described with λ ≈−9×10−5 erg/G2 cm2 for L Ti =0.3nm and λ ≈−3.3×10−5 erg/G2 cm2 for L Ti =0.5nm at T > T comp. As mentioned above, the surface non-collinear phase may noticeably influence the formation of a non-collinear state in whole sample. Therefore, there it makes no sense to compare the experimental and calculated H cr(T) dependences at T < T comp for L Ti =0.5nm. It can be seen for the cases under consideration that H cr(T) shows a linear variation, due to the linear behaviour of M GdCo(T) in the temperature interval described.

Let us return now to the temperature dependence of H c, which reaches its maximum near T comp (Fig. 7). Other authors [33] observed a local minimum of coercivity of the [Fe(4nm)/Gd(4nm)]4 multilayer around the T comp indicating a difference between this multilayered structure and a homogeneous ferrimagnet (for the latter the coercivity diverges as 1/M s when the ferrimagnet goes through the T comp due to the vanishing net magnetic moment). The comparison of these two results confirms that, unlike RE/TM multilayers, Co/(Gd–Co) multilayers can be considered to be very similar to homogenous ferrimagnets.

Regarding the Ti role in these multilayers, the results described above show that the interlayer interaction depends strongly on the non-magnetic spacer thickness. In effect, spacers with a thickness of 0.3nm have practically no influence on the exchange between Co and Gd–Co layers: samples with L Ti of 0 and 0.3nm have similar hysteresis loops, H c(T) and H cr(T) dependencies. The clearest indicator of there being no change in interaction is the fact that the value of λ is almost the same in both samples. The 0.5nm spacer strongly reduces the interaction. It is clear that a spacer with a thickness of 1.5nm completely destroys the interlayer coupling. As we mentioned above, the hysteresis loop (Fig. 4) can be interpreted as arising from sequential independent magnetizations of Gd–Co and Co layers. Thus, the introduction of Ti as a non-magnetic spacer is an effective way to influence the interlayer coupling.

Such a feature of the interlayer coupling can be related to structure transition of Ti spacers, as L Ti increases. It can be assumed that the Ti layers of a nominal thickness of 0.5nm deposited by the sputtering technique are not continuous, but display an island-like structure. The persistence of small areas of direct contact between Co and Gd–Co layers allows some interaction to take place. At low thickness, the Ti atoms do not form even an island-like structure, but rather behave like the additional atoms which decrease the density of magnetic atoms at the interfaces. Spacers of greater thickness can have higher continuity and completely destroy interlayer coupling at certain spacer thickness.

In summary, the [Co/Ti/Gd0.36Co0.64/Ti]4/Co multilayered structure exhibits macroscopic ferrimagnetic behaviour manifested by: (i) the sign change of the TMOKE hysteresis loops, (ii) the increase of coercivity and (iii) the decrease of the field of spin-orientation transition from the collinear to a non-collinear state near the compensation temperature. The temperature dependence of the spin-orientation transition field can be described in the framework of molecular field theory for isotropic ferrimagnets. The introduction of Ti spacers with a thickness of 0.3nm has practically no influence on interlayer exchange interaction between Co and Gd–Co layers, whereas those of 1.5nm completely inhibit interlayer coupling.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by RFBR (grant 04-02-16485a), Spanish projects MAT2001-0082, MCT-03-MAT-06407 (M. Tejedor and A. Fernandez), “Ramon y Cajal” Fellowship of Basque Country University UPV-EHU and Spanish MEC (G.V. Kurlyandskaya). The authors wish to thank Dr. I. Orúe for the assistance with AFM measurements and SQUID measurements at sharing services of UPV-EHU.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Magnetic Multilayers and Giant Magnetoresistance: Fundamentals and Applications
The Handbook of Binary Phase Diagrams
Orientational Transition in Rare Earth Magnets
